title: Shioda’s Conjecture on Unirationality
speaker: Ben Church
abstract: In characteristic zero, Castelnuovo proved that every unirational surface is rational. In positive characteristic, this fails dramatically: there exist many non-rational, often even general-type, surfaces that are nevertheless unirational. In 1977, Shioda conjectured that such phenomena are completely explained by the Galois representations—i.e., a simply-connected surface is unirational if and only if it is supersingular. In this talk, I will present a counterexample to this conjecture. The construction requires a new obstruction, inspired by ideas from the study of the hyperbolicity of complex varieties.

proof_stages.label: Unirationality and classical obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines geometric unirationality, recalls Castelnuovo's characteristic-zero surface criterion, and explains fundamental-group and tensor-form obstructions together with their failure under inseparable maps in characteristic p.

proof_stages.label: Shioda supersingularity conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Shioda--Katsura's Fermat-surface theorem relates unirationality to Frobenius eigenvalues and algebraic H^2, motivating the definition of Shioda supersingularity and the conjecture that, for simply connected surfaces, it is equivalent to unirationality; the easy implication from unirationality to supersingularity is proved by resolving a rational map from P^2.

proof_stages.label: Target counterexample and need for a new obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The announced theorem requires a finite-field surface with trivial étale fundamental group and algebraic H^2 that is nevertheless not unirational, so the central task becomes finding a new obstruction to parametrization by P^2.

proof_stages.label: Symmetric differentials and foliated zero divisors
proof_stages.summary: Inspired by Bogomolov's characteristic-zero method, a symmetric differential is used to force rational or elliptic curves to lift to the zero divisor Z(ω) in the projectivized tangent bundle, where their lifts are leaves of a natural foliation; considering one curve at a time preserves derivative information despite characteristic-p inseparability.

proof_stages.label: p-curvature and quotient by foliation
proof_stages.summary: For a foliation in characteristic p, nonzero p-curvature confines leaves to its vanishing locus, while zero p-curvature gives an inseparable same-dimensional quotient by the foliation from which leaves pull back, lowering their degree by a factor of p.

proof_stages.label: Infinite descent obstruction to rational curves
proof_stages.summary: Iterating the lift-to-Z(ω), quotient-by-foliation construction either stops with finitely many exceptional curves or runs indefinitely, forcing propagated rational curves to have degrees divisible by arbitrarily high powers of p and hence proving non-coverage by rational curves.

proof_stages.label: Self-similar product-quotient surfaces with diagonal forms
proof_stages.summary: For product-quotient surfaces obtained from two G-curves, diagonal symmetric forms make the quotient step a partial Frobenius twist, so over finite fields the descent becomes self-similar; a numerical intersection criterion and large perfect G-Belyi groups provide checkable families not covered by rational curves.

proof_stages.label: Hurwitz--Shimura arithmetic realization of the counterexample
proof_stages.summary: A genus-14 Hurwitz/Shimura curve with automorphism group PSL2(F13) and Jacobian isogenous to powers of an elliptic curve supplies quotients verified to be simply connected and to satisfy the diagonal-form obstruction, while Elkies' theorem gives infinitely many supersingular primes at which H^2 is algebraic, yielding counterexamples to Shioda's conjecture.

examples.name: Purely inseparable surfaces z^p=f(x,y)
examples.note: The equation z^p=f(x,y) is used to show concretely how a characteristic-p parametrization can be unirational while annihilating many differential forms by inseparability.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes the surface equation, makes the substitutions x=s^p and y=t^p, checks the equation in characteristic p, and interprets this as a unirational parametrization.

passes.reason: The purely inseparable p-th-root cover has collapsed differential behavior and is used to show tensor/pluricanonical forms do not obstruct unirationality in characteristic p.
A purely inseparable p-th-root cover has Frobenius-collapsed differentials and is used to show the characteristic-zero tensor-form obstruction to unirationality fails.
Purely inseparable p-cover with collapsed differentials used against the tensor-form obstruction
A pth-power equation gives a purely inseparable zero-differential cover used to refute characteristic-zero tensor-form obstructions.
purely inseparable p-th-power covers collapse differentials and are used to refute the characteristic-zero tensor-form obstruction to unirationality.
the p-th-power equation gives a purely inseparable, differential-collapsing cover used to refute characteristic-zero form/Kodaira obstructions to unirationality
Used to show tensor/pluricanonical-form obstructions to unirationality fail in characteristic p, and the p-th-root equation is Frobenius-collapsed/purely inseparable.
Used to show the characteristic-zero tensor-form obstruction fails, via a Frobenius-collapsed purely inseparable cover that can be unirational despite arbitrary general type.
Purely inseparable p-th-power covers are used to refute the characteristic-zero tensor-form obstruction by collapsing differentials.
A Frobenius/purely inseparable cover with zero differential, used to show tensor-form and Kodaira-dimension obstructions fail in characteristic p.
Used to show tensor/pluricanonical forms and general type do not obstruct unirationality in characteristic p, and the p-th-power equation gives a purely inseparable differential collapse.
They refute the characteristic-zero tensor-form obstruction via a purely inseparable, differential-collapsed cover.

examples.name: Shioda--Katsura Fermat surfaces
examples.note: Fermat surfaces exhibit the congruence condition equating unirationality with algebraic H^2, visibly motivating Shioda's supersingularity conjecture; later they illustrate why abelian product-quotient cases evade the new obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: The Fermat surfaces are stated as Shioda--Katsura examples with their congruence criterion and cohomological characterization, but no concrete computation is carried out on a particular surface.

passes.reason: Fermat surfaces are highly regular symmetric hypersurfaces and are used to exhibit arbitrarily general-type surfaces that are nevertheless unirational at many primes.

The Fermat surfaces are highly symmetric and, at the Shioda--Katsura primes, supersingular with maximal algebraic H2, while showing that very general-type surfaces can still be unirational for many primes.
Maximally symmetric Fermat hypersurfaces with maximal algebraic H2 in the congruence cases used against general-type obstruction intuition
The Fermat form is maximally regular and its supersingular reductions have maximal Picard rank while refuting finite-bad-prime/general-type intuition.
Fermat surfaces are maximally regular and, in the Shioda--Katsura congruence cases, have maximal algebraic H2 while still illustrating failure of general-type intuition.

Fermat surfaces are highly symmetric and, in the Shioda--Katsura congruence cases, supersingular with maximal algebraic H^2, serving as high-general-type unirational examples against naive characteristic-zero expectations
Used as a motivating positive family for Shioda’s supersingularity criterion, with Fermat symmetry and, in the relevant congruence cases, maximal Picard/supersingular behavior.
Used as motivating positive evidence for Shioda’s picture; in the congruence cases the Fermat surface is supersingular with maximal algebraic H^2 and has the highly regular Fermat symmetry.
Fermat surfaces are highly symmetric and the congruence cases have maximal algebraic H2, but they serve as motivating evidence for Shioda's criterion.
Fermat surfaces are highly diagonal-symmetric and in the Shioda--Katsura cases have maximal algebraic H^2, used to refute characteristic-zero general-type expectations.

Used to refute the expectation that high-degree general-type reductions should rarely be unirational, while Fermat surfaces are maximally regular and the relevant reductions are supersingular with maximal algebraic H2.
They show high-degree general-type Fermat surfaces can still be unirational at many primes, and the supersingular cases have maximal algebraic H2.

examples.name: Godeaux-type quotient warning
examples.note: Godeaux-like quotients are mentioned to illustrate why replacing trivial fundamental group by finite fundamental group gives a false variant of Shioda's conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: The Godeaux-type quotients are invoked only as a warning that a finite-fundamental-group version of the conjecture fails, without working through the quotient.

passes.reason: The Godeaux-type quotient is a standard finite-quotient construction used as a warning counterexample to replacing trivial fundamental group by finite fundamental group.
Godeaux-type quotients are cohomologically minimal boundary-type general-type surfaces and are cited to refute the finite-pi1 version of Shioda's conjecture.
Cohomologically minimal Godeaux-type quotient used against the finite-pi1 version
Godeaux-type quotients have deliberately minimal cohomological invariants and are used to defeat the finite-fundamental-group version.
the Godeaux-style finite quotient is a systematic quotient trick used to show the finite-fundamental-group version of the conjecture is false.
the Godeaux-type finite quotient cohomologically collapses the cover and is cited as the reason the finite-pi_1 variant of the conjecture is false
Used as a warning that the finite-fundamental-group version of Shioda’s conjecture is false via quotients that kill the interesting cohomology.
Used as a warning counterexample to replacing trivial pi_1 by finite pi_1, with Godeaux-type quotients lying on the low-invariant boundary of general-type surfaces.
The Godeaux-type finite quotient collapses the interesting cohomology and warns against replacing trivial pi1 by finite pi1.
A standard Godeaux-type quotient is invoked as a warning counterexample to the finite-fundamental-group version of the conjecture.
Used as a warning that replacing trivial pi1 by finite pi1 makes Shioda's formulation false, and Godeaux-type surfaces lie on the minimal/boundary side of general-type surface geography.
The quotient is cited as a counterexample to the finite-pi1 version of Shioda’s conjecture, but is a systematic quotient trick.

examples.name: Affine partial-Frobenius foliation model
examples.note: The map A^1→A^2 tangent to the foliation generated by ∂/∂y shows that tangency forces one coordinate to be a p-th power, revealing the partial-Frobenius quotient and degree drop used in the descent.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly analyzes the affine model t↦(f(t),g(t)), computes its tangent vector, derives f'=0 iff f is a function of t^p, and identifies the partial-Frobenius map.

passes.reason: The partial-Frobenius affine model has one infinitesimal direction collapsed by Frobenius and serves only as an illustrative local model for the foliation quotient.
The affine partial-Frobenius model is a deliberately collapsed inseparable coordinate model illustrating quotienting by a foliation, not refuting a claim.
Flat coordinate foliation with a one-coordinate Frobenius tangent collapse used as a local model
The partial Frobenius kills one tangent direction and serves as a local model for foliation quotients.
the partial Frobenius affine model is inseparable and collapses one coordinate direction, serving only as an explanatory local model.
the affine model is a partial Frobenius with a rank-collapsing inseparable direction, used illustratively rather than adversarially
Introduced as an explanatory local model, with partial Frobenius collapsing one coordinate inseparably.
Used only as a local explanatory model, whose partial Frobenius map is inseparable and derivative-collapsed in one coordinate.
The partial-Frobenius model is an inseparable coordinate-collapse used only to illustrate foliation descent.
The affine partial-Frobenius model collapses one coordinate inseparably and serves only to illustrate the foliation quotient mechanism.
Presented as a local explanatory model for foliation quotients, with partial Frobenius collapsing one coordinate inseparably.
It is an illustrative local model whose partial Frobenius is inseparable and tangent-direction-collapsing.

examples.name: Low-genus Hurwitz curves that fail
examples.note: The Klein quartic and the genus-7 Hurwitz curve are mentioned as earlier trial cases that did not satisfy all the needed arithmetic and geometric conditions, illustrating the search leading to genus 14.
examples.engagement_reason: The low-genus Hurwitz curves such as the Klein quartic in genus 3 and the genus-7 case are only cited as attempts that did not work, with no details checked on them.

passes.reason: Hurwitz curves are maximally automorphic, hence extremal and symmetric, and the low-genus cases are cited as failed candidate cases for the construction.
The low-genus Hurwitz curves are maximally automorphic and highly symmetric, but are used as failed candidates showing the Hurwitz strategy does not work automatically.
Automorphism-maximal Hurwitz curves cited as failed low-genus candidates for the construction
Hurwitz curves attain the automorphism bound and the low-genus cases are cited as failed candidates for the construction.
low-genus Hurwitz curves attain the maximal automorphism bound and are cited as failed candidates for the construction.
Hurwitz curves attain the maximal automorphism bound and the low-genus cases are cited as failed candidates showing the construction is not automatic
They are cited as failed candidate cases for the construction, while Hurwitz curves attain the maximal automorphism bound and are therefore highly symmetric.
Used as failed test cases for the Hurwitz-curve strategy, while Hurwitz curves are extremal and highly symmetric by attaining the 84(g−1) automorphism bound.
The low-genus Hurwitz curves are maximally automorphic cases used as failed candidates for the construction.
The low-genus Hurwitz curves are maximally symmetric curves attaining the Hurwitz automorphism bound, here serving as failed candidate cases for the construction.
Mentioned as failed search candidates rather than refutations, while Hurwitz curves attain the maximal automorphism bound 84(g-1).
The genus 3 and 7 Hurwitz curves are failed trial cases for the construction and Hurwitz curves maximize automorphism order.
